title: Palindrome partitioning
description: Partition string into all palindrome substrings

examples.explanation: Start: s="aab", start=0, path=[]. Iterate end from 1 to 3. end=1: substr="a", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a"]. Recursive call: start=1, path=["a"]. Iterate end from 2 to 3. end=2: substr="a", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a","a"]. Recursive call: start=2, path=["a","a"]. Iterate end from 3 to 3. end=3: substr="b", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a","a","b"]. Recursive call: start=3, path=["a","a","b"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a","a","b"]]. Backtrack: remove "b", path=["a","a"]. Continue loop: no more ends. Backtrack: remove "a", path=["a"]. Continue loop: end=3: substr="ab", palindrome? no. Return to outer call. Backtrack: remove "a", path=[]. Continue loop: end=2: substr="aa", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["aa"]. Recursive call: start=2, path=["aa"]. Iterate end from 3 to 3. end=3: substr="b", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["aa","b"]. Recursive call: start=3, path=["aa","b"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a","a","b"],["aa","b"]]. Backtrack: remove "b", path=["aa"]. Continue loop: no more ends. Backtrack: remove "aa", path=[]. Continue loop: end=3: substr="aab", palindrome? no. End. Result: [["a","a","b"],["aa","b"]].

Start: s="a", start=0, path=[]. Iterate end from 1 to 1. end=1: substr="a", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a"]. Recursive call: start=1, path=["a"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a"]]. Backtrack: remove "a", path=[]. Continue loop: no more ends. Result: [["a"]].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Backtracking O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible partitions of the string by recursively exploring every split point, checking if each substring is a palindrome using a helper function. This is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated palindrome checks and exponential partitions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Palindrome Partitioning via Backtracking
ladders.desc: Generate all possible palindrome partitions of a string using brute-force backtracking.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to explore all possible ways to split the string by recursively trying every possible partition point. At each step, we check if the current substring (from the last partition to the current index) is a palindrome. If it is, we add it to our current partition list and recursively continue with the remaining string. This ensures we only build partitions where every substring is a palindrome. The algorithm works by maintaining a starting index that tracks where the next substring begins. We iterate from this start index to the end of the string, checking each possible end point. For each valid palindrome substring found, we add it to the current partition and recursively call the function with the next starting index. When we reach the end of the string, we add the current partition to the result list. The time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because there are 2^n possible partitions (each character can be a split point or not), and for each partition, we may check palindromes up to O(n) length. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and storing partitions. This connects to L1 by relying on the palindrome checking helper function. Common mistakes include not backtracking properly (forgetting to remove the last added substring) or inefficient palindrome checks. Real code pattern: def partition(s): result = []; def backtrack(start, path): if start == len(s): result.append(path[:]); return; for end in range(start+1, len(s)+1): substr = s[start:end]; if is_palindrome(substr): path.append(substr); backtrack(end, path); path.pop(); backtrack(0, []); return result.

examples.trace: Start: s="aab", start=0, path=[]. Iterate end from 1 to 3. end=1: substr="a", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a"]. Recursive call: start=1, path=["a"]. Iterate end from 2 to 3. end=2: substr="a", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a","a"]. Recursive call: start=2, path=["a","a"]. Iterate end from 3 to 3. end=3: substr="b", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a","a","b"]. Recursive call: start=3, path=["a","a","b"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a","a","b"]]. Backtrack: remove "b", path=["a","a"]. Continue loop: no more ends. Backtrack: remove "a", path=["a"]. Continue loop: end=3: substr="ab", palindrome? no. Return to outer call. Backtrack: remove "a", path=[]. Continue loop: end=2: substr="aa", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["aa"]. Recursive call: start=2, path=["aa"]. Iterate end from 3 to 3. end=3: substr="b", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["aa","b"]. Recursive call: start=3, path=["aa","b"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a","a","b"],["aa","b"]]. Backtrack: remove "b", path=["aa"]. Continue loop: no more ends. Backtrack: remove "aa", path=[]. Continue loop: end=3: substr="aab", palindrome? no. End. Result: [["a","a","b"],["aa","b"]].

Start: s="a", start=0, path=[]. Iterate end from 1 to 1. end=1: substr="a", palindrome? yes. Add to path: ["a"]. Recursive call: start=1, path=["a"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a"]]. Backtrack: remove "a", path=[]. Continue loop: no more ends. Result: [["a"]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Palindrome Checking Function
ladders.desc: Determine if a given string is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, so we can compare characters from both ends moving towards the center. This is a fundamental operation needed for the partitioning algorithm to validate substrings. The algorithm uses two pointers: one at the start and one at the end of the string. While the start pointer is less than the end pointer, compare the characters at these positions. If any pair differs, the string is not a palindrome; otherwise, move the pointers inward. Continue until the pointers meet or cross, confirming the string is a palindrome. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the string, as we may check up to k/2 pairs. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra space. This connects to L0 by being called repeatedly in the backtracking process to filter valid partitions. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings (which are palindromes by definition) or off-by-one errors in pointer movement. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): left = 0; right = len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: s="racecar", left=0, right=6. left<right? 0<6 yes. s[0]='r', s[6]='r', equal? yes. left=1, right=5. left<right? 1<5 yes. s[1]='a', s[5]='a', equal? yes. left=2, right=4. left<right? 2<4 yes. s[2]='c', s[4]='c', equal? yes. left=3, right=3. left<right? 3<3 no. Return true.
s="hello", left=0, right=4. left<right? 0<4 yes. s[0]='h', s[4]='o', equal? no. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking for Partition Generation
ladders.desc: Generate all possible partitions of a string using recursion and backtracking.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to explore all split combinations by recursively dividing the string at each possible point, akin to a depth-first search through partition space. This technique avoids storing all partitions explicitly until completion by building them incrementally. The algorithm starts with an empty partition and a pointer at the beginning of the string. For each possible end index from the start pointer to the end of the string, we consider the substring from start to end. We then recursively explore partitions for the remaining string (from end to the end). When we reach the end of the string, we save the current partition. Backtracking involves removing the last added substring to try other possibilities. Time complexity is O(2^n) for generating all partitions, as each character can be a split point or not. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion depth and current partition storage. This connects to L0 by forming the main recursive structure, with L1's palindrome check integrated as a filter. Common mistakes include not resetting state correctly after recursion or missing base cases. Real code pattern: def generate_partitions(s, start, path, result): if start == len(s): result.append(path[:]); return; for end in range(start+1, len(s)+1): substr = s[start:end]; path.append(substr); generate_partitions(s, end, path, result); path.pop().

examples.trace: Start: s="ab", start=0, path=[], result=[]. Iterate end from 1 to 2. end=1: substr="a". Add to path: ["a"]. Recursive call: start=1, path=["a"]. Iterate end from 2 to 2. end=2: substr="b". Add to path: ["a","b"]. Recursive call: start=2, path=["a","b"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a","b"]]. Backtrack: remove "b", path=["a"]. Continue loop: no more ends. Backtrack: remove "a", path=[]. Continue outer loop: end=2: substr="ab". Add to path: ["ab"]. Recursive call: start=2, path=["ab"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["a","b"],["ab"]]. Backtrack: remove "ab", path=[]. End. Result: [["a","b"],["ab"]].

Start: s="x", start=0, path=[], result=[]. Iterate end from 1 to 1. end=1: substr="x". Add to path: ["x"]. Recursive call: start=1, path=["x"]. start==len(s)? yes. Add to result: [["x"]]. Backtrack: remove "x", path=[]. End. Result: [["x"]].

ladders.title: Building Block — String Slicing and Substring Iteration
ladders.desc: Iterate through all possible substrings of a string from a given start index.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to generate all contiguous substrings starting from a fixed position, which is essential for exploring partition points in backtracking. This involves using a loop to vary the end index, creating substrings of increasing length. The algorithm takes a string and a start index. It iterates the end index from start+1 to the length of the string. For each end index, it extracts the substring from start to end (exclusive of end in 0-based indexing). This produces all possible substrings that begin at the start index. Time complexity is O(n) per start index, where n is the length of the string from start to end, as we create up to n substrings. Space complexity is O(n) for storing substrings, but typically we process them one at a time. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism to try each possible split in the backtracking loop. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (e.g., using inclusive vs exclusive bounds) or not handling empty substrings. Real code pattern: def iterate_substrings(s, start): for end in range(start+1, len(s)+1): substr = s[start:end]; # process substr.

examples.trace: s="abc", start=0. Iterate end from 1 to 3. end=1: substr=s[0:1]="a". end=2: substr=s[0:2]="ab". end=3: substr=s[0:3]="abc". Result: processed substrings: "a", "ab", "abc".
s="hello", start=2. Iterate end from 3 to 5. end=3: substr=s[2:3]="l". end=4: substr=s[2:4]="ll". end=5: substr=s[2:5]="llo". Result: processed substrings: "l", "ll", "llo".

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Two-Poiter Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare characters from both ends of a string using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to check symmetry in a string by simultaneously examining characters from the start and end, moving inward. This is a fundamental technique used in palindrome checking and other string algorithms. The algorithm initializes two pointers: left at index 0 and right at the last index (length-1). While left is less than right, compare the characters at these indices. If they are equal, increment left and decrement right to move towards the center. If any pair is not equal, the process can stop early indicating asymmetry. This efficiently reduces the number of comparisons needed. Time complexity is O(n/2) in the worst case, which simplifies to O(n), where n is the string length. Space complexity is O(1) as it uses only a constant amount of extra memory for pointers. This connects to L3 by being the core operation in substring palindrome validation. Common mistakes include not updating pointers correctly or mishandling edge cases like empty strings. Real code pattern: left = 0; right = len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: s="radar", left=0, right=4. left<right? 0<4 yes. s[0]='r', s[4]='r', equal? yes. left=1, right=3. left<right? 1<3 yes. s[1]='a', s[3]='a', equal? yes. left=2, right=2. left<right? 2<2 no. Return true.
s="apple", left=0, right=4. left<right? 0<4 yes. s[0]='a', s[4]='e', equal? no. Return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Length
ladders.desc: Access characters in a string using indices and determine its length.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters where each character has a position, starting from 0. This foundational concept is crucial for manipulating strings in algorithms. In most programming languages, strings are indexed starting at 0 for the first character, up to length-1 for the last. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains. To access a character, use the syntax s[index]. For example, in "cat", s[0] is 'c', s[1] is 'a', s[2] is 't', and the length is 3. This allows iteration and comparison of characters. Time complexity for accessing by index is typically O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the operation itself. This connects to L4 by enabling pointer-based operations on strings. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: s = "example"; first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[len(s)-1]; length = len(s).

examples.trace: String: "hello". Index 0: character 'h'. Index 1: character 'e'. Index 2: character 'l'. Index 3: character 'l'. Index 4: character 'o'. Length: count characters: h,e,l,l,o → 5.
String: "a". Index 0: character 'a'. Length: count characters: a → 1.

approaches.name: Approach 3: DP Iterative Partitioning O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming to build a DP array where dp[i] stores all palindrome partitions for the prefix s[0:i], iteratively extending from smaller substrings. This avoids recursion and systematically computes partitions based on precomputed palindrome information.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Palindrome Partitioning with DP Iterative Partitioning
ladders.desc: Complete solution to partition a string into all palindrome substrings using dynamic programming with O(n²) time and space.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the palindrome partitioning problem by building a DP array where dp[i] stores all palindrome partitions for the prefix s[0:i] (i.e., first i characters). The intuition is to compute partitions iteratively from smaller substrings to larger ones, avoiding recursion. We precompute a 2D boolean array isPal[i][j] that indicates whether substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome, which takes O(n²) time using expansion from centers or DP. Then, for each position i from 0 to n-1, we iterate j from 0 to i. If isPal[j][i] is true (meaning s[j:i+1] is a palindrome), we append this substring to every partition in dp[j-1] (if j>0) or start a new partition (if j==0), and add the resulting partitions to dp[i]. This ensures all partitions are built systematically. The time complexity is O(n²) for precomputation plus O(n²) for partitioning, dominated by O(n²). Space is O(n²) for isPal and dp arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of building partitions from palindrome checks. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, forgetting to handle empty partitions for j==0, or inefficient palindrome checking. Real code pattern: Initialize dp as list of lists, precompute isPal, then nested loops to build partitions.

examples.trace: Step 1: Precompute isPal for "aab" (n=3). isPal[0][0]=True ("a"), isPal[1][1]=True ("a"), isPal[2][2]=True ("b"), isPal[0][1]=True ("aa"), others False. Step 2: Initialize dp[0]=[["a"]], dp[1]=[], dp[2]=[]. Step 3: For i=0: j=0, isPal[0][0]=True, j==0 so add ["a"] to dp[0]. dp[0]=[["a"]]. Step 4: For i=1: j=0, isPal[0][1]=True, j==0 so add ["aa"] to dp[1]; j=1, isPal[1][1]=True, append "a" to partitions in dp[0] -> ["a","a"], add to dp[1]. dp[1]=[["aa"],["a","a"]]. Step 5: For i=2: j=0, isPal[0][2]=False; j=1, isPal[1][2]=False; j=2, isPal[2][2]=True, append "b" to partitions in dp[1] -> ["aa","b"] and ["a","a","b"], add to dp[2]. dp[2]=[["aa","b"],["a","a","b"]]. Output dp[2].
Step 1: Precompute isPal for "a" (n=1). isPal[0][0]=True. Step 2: Initialize dp[0]=[]. Step 3: For i=0: j=0, isPal[0][0]=True, j==0 so add ["a"] to dp[0]. dp[0]=[["a"]]. Output dp[0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building Partitions from Palindrome Checks
ladders.desc: The core function that iteratively constructs palindrome partitions using precomputed palindrome information.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the DP iterative partitioning approach. Given a string s of length n, a precomputed 2D boolean array isPal where isPal[i][j] indicates if substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome, and a DP array dp where dp[i] will store all palindrome partitions for prefix s[0:i+1], this function populates dp. The intuition is to build partitions incrementally: for each ending index i, we consider all possible starting indices j from 0 to i. If s[j:i+1] is a palindrome (isPal[j][i] is true), then we can form partitions ending at i by taking all partitions from dp[j-1] (if j>0) and appending s[j:i+1], or if j==0, we start a new partition with just this substring. This ensures every partition is valid because each added substring is a palindrome. Step-by-step: Initialize dp as a list of empty lists for i from 0 to n-1. For i from 0 to n-1, for j from 0 to i, if isPal[j][i] is true, then if j==0, add [s[j:i+1]] to dp[i]; else, for each partition in dp[j-1], create a new partition by appending s[j:i+1] and add to dp[i]. Time complexity: O(n²) in worst case as nested loops over i and j, and copying partitions can add factor but bounded by output size. Space: O(n²) for dp. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called within the full solution after precomputing isPal. Common mistakes: Not initializing dp correctly, missing the j==0 case, or inefficient list copying. Real code pattern: Nested loops with condition checks and list comprehensions.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0: isPal[0][0]=True, j==0 so add ["a"] to dp[0]. dp[0]=[["a"]]. Step 2: i=1, j=0: isPal[0][1]=False; j=1: isPal[1][1]=True, j>0 so take partitions from dp[0]=[["a"]], append "b" -> ["a","b"], add to dp[1]. dp[1]=[["a","b"]].

Step 1: i=0, j=0: isPal[0][0]=True, j==0 so add ["a"] to dp[0]. dp[0]=[["a"]]. Step 2: i=1, j=0: isPal[0][1]=True, j==0 so add ["aa"] to dp[1]; j=1: isPal[1][1]=True, j>0 so take partitions from dp[0]=[["a"]], append "a" -> ["a","a"], add to dp[1]. dp[1]=[["aa"],["a","a"]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Programming for Partition Accumulation
ladders.desc: The central technique of using DP to store and build palindrome partitions iteratively.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the dynamic programming recurrence that defines how partitions are accumulated. The key insight is that to find all palindrome partitions for a prefix ending at index i, we can combine partitions from earlier prefixes with new palindrome substrings. Specifically, let dp[i] be the list of all palindrome partitions for s[0:i+1]. Then, dp[i] can be computed by considering all j from 0 to i: if substring s[j:i+1] is a palindrome, then we take all partitions in dp[j-1] (for j>0) and append s[j:i+1] to each, or if j==0, we use the partition consisting solely of s[j:i+1]. This works because any partition ending at i must end with some palindrome substring s[j:i+1], and the remaining part s[0:j] must be partitioned into palindromes, which is exactly what dp[j-1] stores. The intuition is breaking the problem into overlapping subproblems: partitioning a string reduces to partitioning its prefixes. This avoids recursion by building bottom-up. Step-by-step: Initialize dp array of size n with empty lists. For i from 0 to n-1, for j from 0 to i, check if s[j:i+1] is palindrome (assume precomputed). If true, if j==0, add [s[j:i+1]] to dp[i]; else, for each partition in dp[j-1], create new partition by appending s[j:i+1] and add to dp[i]. Time: O(n²) for loops, but palindrome checking adds cost; with precomputation, overall O(n²). Space: O(n²) for dp. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Incorrect indexing leading to off-by-one errors, not handling empty string case. Real code pattern: Use list appends and comprehensions.

examples.trace: Assume palindrome checks: s[0:1]="a" is palindrome, s[1:2]="b" is palindrome, s[0:2]="ab" is not. Step 1: i=0, j=0: s[0:1] palindrome, j==0 so dp[0]=[["a"]]. Step 2: i=1, j=0: s[0:2] not palindrome; j=1: s[1:2] palindrome, j>0 so take dp[0]=[["a"]], append "b" -> ["a","b"], dp[1]=[["a","b"]].

Assume palindrome checks: s[0:1]="a" palindrome, s[1:2]="a" palindrome, s[0:2]="aa" palindrome. Step 1: i=0, j=0: s[0:1] palindrome, j==0 so dp[0]=[["a"]]. Step 2: i=1, j=0: s[0:2] palindrome, j==0 so add ["aa"] to dp[1]; j=1: s[1:2] palindrome, j>0 so take dp[0]=[["a"]], append "a" -> ["a","a"], add to dp[1]. dp[1]=[["aa"],["a","a"]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Precomputing Palindrome Substrings
ladders.desc: Creating a 2D array that stores whether each substring is a palindrome, essential for efficient DP.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves precomputing a boolean 2D array isPal where isPal[i][j] is true if substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. This is crucial for the DP partitioning approach to avoid repeatedly checking palindromes, reducing time complexity. The intuition is that palindrome checking can be done efficiently using dynamic programming or expansion from centers. For DP: isPal[i][j] is true if s[i] == s[j] and (j-i <= 2 or isPal[i+1][j-1] is true). This means a substring is palindrome if its ends match and the inner substring is also palindrome (for length > 2). We compute this bottom-up by iterating over substring lengths. Step-by-step: Initialize isPal as n x n false array. Set single characters as true: isPal[i][i] = true for all i. For length L from 2 to n, for i from 0 to n-L, j = i+L-1, set isPal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and (L==2 or isPal[i+1][j-1]). Time: O(n²) as nested loops over lengths and starts. Space: O(n²) for the array. Connection to L2: This precomputed array is used in the core logic to quickly check if s[j:i+1] is palindrome. Common mistakes: Incorrect indices in loops, not handling length 2 case separately. Real code pattern: Nested loops with condition checks.

examples.trace: n=2. Step 1: Initialize isPal 2x2 false. Step 2: Set isPal[0][0]=True, isPal[1][1]=True. Step 3: Length L=2, i=0, j=1: s[0]='a', s[1]='b', not equal so isPal[0][1]=False.
n=2. Step 1: Initialize isPal 2x2 false. Step 2: Set isPal[0][0]=True, isPal[1][1]=True. Step 3: Length L=2, i=0, j=1: s[0]='a', s[1]='a', equal and L==2 so isPal[0][1]=True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if a Substring is a Palindrome
ladders.desc: Determining whether a given substring of a string is a palindrome, a fundamental operation in the problem.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation checks if a substring s[i:j+1] (inclusive indices) is a palindrome, meaning it reads the same forwards and backwards. The intuition is to compare characters from the ends towards the center. A simple way is to use two pointers: start at i and end at j, move inward while characters match; if all match, it's a palindrome. Step-by-step: Initialize left = i, right = j. While left < right, if s[left] != s[right], return false; else, increment left and decrement right. If loop completes, return true. Time: O(k) where k = j-i+1 is substring length. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in precomputing the palindrome array; optimizing it to O(1) via precomputation improves overall efficiency. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, not handling empty or single-character substrings correctly (they are palindromes). Real code pattern: While loop with character comparisons.

examples.trace: Substring s[0:7]="racecar". Step 1: left=0, right=6, s[0]='r', s[6]='r', match. Step 2: left=1, right=5, s[1]='a', s[5]='a', match. Step 3: left=2, right=4, s[2]='c', s[4]='c', match. Step 4: left=3, right=3, loop ends as left >= right. Return true.
Substring s[1:4]="ell". Step 1: left=1, right=3, s[1]='e', s[3]='l', do not match. Return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding the definition and properties of palindromes, the simplest prerequisite for the problem.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence of characters that reads the same forwards and backwards. In the context of strings, this means the string is symmetric about its center. For example, "racecar" reversed is "racecar", so it's a palindrome. Key properties: Single characters are always palindromes (e.g., "a"). The empty string is considered a palindrome by convention. A palindrome can have even or odd length; for even length, each pair of symmetric characters matches; for odd length, the middle character can be anything. To check if a string is a palindrome, you can compare it to its reverse: if string == string[::-1] in Python, it's a palindrome. Alternatively, use two pointers starting at the ends and moving inward, comparing characters. This concept is foundational because the problem requires partitioning a string into substrings that are all palindromes. Understanding palindromes helps in designing checks and algorithms. Connection to L4: This concept is applied in the basic operation of checking substrings. Common mistakes: Forgetting that single characters are palindromes, or mishandling case sensitivity (problem usually assumes case-sensitive). Real code pattern: Simple comparison or loop.

examples.trace: String "madam". Reverse is "madam". Since original equals reverse, it is a palindrome. Alternatively, compare characters: index 0 'm' vs index 4 'm' match, index 1 'a' vs index 3 'a' match, index 2 'd' vs itself match. All match, so true.
String "hello". Reverse is "olleh". Since original does not equal reverse, it is not a palindrome. Alternatively, compare characters: index 0 'h' vs index 4 'o' do not match, so false.
